title: A Semantic Parsing Algorithm toSolve Linear Ordering Problems
authors: Maha Alkhairy, Vincent Homer, Brendan O‚ÄôConnorUniversity of Massachusetts Amherst, Amherst, MA, USAmalkhairy@umass.edu
abstract: We develop an algorithm to semantically parse linear ordering problems, which require a model to arrange entities using deductive reasoning. Our method takes as input a number of premises and candidate statements, parsing them to a first-order logic of an ordering domain, and then utilizes constraint logic programming to infer the truth of proposed statements about the ordering. Our semantic parser transforms Heim and Kratzer (1998)‚Äôs syntax-based compositional formal semantic rules to a computational algorithm. This transformation involves introducing abstract types and templates based on their rules, and introduces a dynamic component to interpret entities within a contextual framework. Our symbolic system, the Formal Semantic Logic Inferer (FSLI), is applied to answer multiple choice questions in BIG-bench‚Äôs logical_deduction multiple choice problems, achieving perfect accuracy, compared to 67.06% for the best-performing LLM (GPT-4) and 87.63% for the hybrid system Logic-LM.  These promising results demonstrate the benefit of developing a semantic parsing algorithm driven by first-order logic constructs.

paragraphs: Natural language processing methods have made impressive recent advances in various tasks. However, they continue to struggle with inferential and logical reasoning, which often requires the incorporation of computational logic.

A class of inferential and logical reasoning is the problem of linear ordering. BIG-bench Srivastava et al. (2023)111https://github.com/google/BIG-bench/tree/main/bigbench/benchmark_tasks/logical_deduction is an LLM benchmark that includes a ‚Äòlogical_deduction‚Äô task that focuses on linear ordering. It asks a model to choose the correct multiple choice statement about an ordering of items, given a description of ordering conditions among them. An example, further explained in Section 4, is:

These problems are constructed so that there is exactly one unambiguously correct response, which can be derived through logical deduction. This task, as part of BIG-bench‚Äôs evaluation suite, is widely used for model evaluation Pan et al. (2023); Schaeffer et al. (2023); Suzgun et al. (2022).

Several NLP approaches have been proposed to solve logic deduction problems, including methods based on large language models OpenAI et al. (2023); Touvron et al. (2023); Anil et al. (2023), hybrid approaches that combine logic inference engines with LLMs Pan et al. (2023), and those based on Combinatory Categorial Grammar (CCG) syntactic-semantic parsing Steedman (2019). While these methods show promise, they also have limitations (detailed in Section 2):
‚Ä¢ LLMs are pattern-based and prone to logical inconsistencies.
‚Ä¢ Hybrid systems improve accuracy but add complexity and reduce flexibility.
‚Ä¢ CCG parsing suffers from high computational cost and ambiguity.

Given the challenges of existing approaches, it is essential to explore structured alternatives that prioritize logical consistency. A conventional approach for solving logical deduction problems is to adopt symbolic systems that incorporate dedicated logic inference modules Cooper et al. (1993); Pereira and Shieber (2002). These systems offer interpretability and generate traceable logical representations, making them suitable foundations for models which solve logical deduction problems. This motivates our decision to build a symbolic system that serves as a benchmark for solving logical deduction problems.

A key requirement for such systems is semantic parsing, which transforms natural language into formal logical representations. While this area has enjoyed much research attention (e.g. Abzianidze and Bos, 2017; Lewis and Steedman, 2014; Misra and Artzi, 2016; Liang et al., 2013; Krishnamurthy and Mitchell, 2012; Cai and Yates, 2013; Artzi et al., 2015; Bos et al., 2004; Xu, 2016; Stanojeviƒá et al., 2021), most prior work in semantic parsing has focused on general language understanding, rather than the specific representational and parsing challenges necessary to perform analytical reasoning in tasks like logical deduction.

To overcome these limitations, we build the Formal Semantic Logic Inferer (FSLI) ‚Äì a symbolic system that combines semantic parsing with a dedicated logic inference module. Unlike hybrid methods, FSLI focuses on precise semantic representation and logical consistency without relying on LLM-generated outputs, through a series of preprocessing, semantic parsing, and logical inference components (Sections 3‚Äì6).

Finally, we evaluate FSLI on the BIG-bench ‚Äòlogical_deduction‚Äô benchmark dataset Srivastava et al. (2023) to assess its semantic parsing proficiency and to ensure the soundness of its logical inferences. Since each problem has a single valid sequence for the arrangement of entities, perfect accuracy is possible, and is in fact achieved by our system (Section 7).
We investigate methodologies relevant to solving the problem of logical deduction in Sections 2.1, 2.2 and 2.3.

subsections.title: 2.1 Large Language Models
paragraphs: The general purpose large language models (LLMs) may naturally be applied to our set of problems, as it can conduct many tasks such as summarization, information extraction, generation of code, content generation, question answering, and solving arithmetic problems.

Marcus and Davis (2019) and Bender and Koller (2020) argue that LLMs have no intrinsic ability for rigorous logical deduction, rigidity, and error propagation. Since LLMs perform pattern matching based on patterns in training data and extrapolate based on those patterns, they may exhibit some rigidity. This rigidity might negatively affect LLMs‚Äô ability to solve reasoning tasks, where the ability to extrapolate and adapt learned patterns to novel problems is essential Marcus and Davis (2019); Bender and Koller (2020); Lake et al. (2017); Zhang et al. (2022).
We illustrate the following example using ChatGPT222https://chat.openai.com/, ChatGPT3.5 and ChatGPT4 in February 2024 and again on ChatGPT4o in February 2025 to test these phenomena.

The given example has no valid solution: if the red desk has to be first, the blue desk has to be last, and the green desk cannot be next to the red desk, then there is no valid ordering. This response demonstrates the possible inability to catch the error in the constraints.

subsections.title: 2.2 Hybrid LLM approach
paragraphs: Pan et al. (2023) integrate logic inference models with LLM logic representation of input problems. This approach uses LLMs to produce the domain, variables, and constraints in first-order logic. For each type of problem, the approach uses a specific solver such as Pyke, and python-constraint333https://github.com/python-constraint/python-constraint. This approach (Logic-LM) outperforms LLMs because of offloading the logical deduction to the solver Pan et al. (2023).

For tasks that are hard for LLMs such as logical_deduction Suzgun et al. (2022), chain of thought prompting (CoT) has been used. In CoT, the user provides the reasoning to be used in solving a problem that is like the one asked.444https://www.promptingguide.ai/techniques/cot Logic-LM also presents results using CoT prompting and demonstrates that this manual support increases the accuracy of LLMs.

subsections.title: 2.3 Combinatory Categorial Grammar
paragraphs: A core prerequisite to logic reasoning is semantic parsing, with combinatory categorial grammar (CCG) Steedman (2000) being a prominent methodology to convert a sentence into its logical representation based on lexicalized syntactic labels Bos et al. (2004); Abzianidze and Bos (2017); Mart√≠nez-G√≥mez et al. (2016). CCG encodes both syntactic and semantic information through lexical annotations. Since CCG models both natural language syntax and semantics in a single representation, it is complex for the task at hand.

In addition, the number of categories in CCG (425) is tenfold that of PoS, thus exploding ambiguity at the outset Hockenmaier and Steedman (2007). Combination rules that could potentially reduce ambiguity are lax Moortgat (1997). The complexity of CCG development is highlighted in Steedman (2000)‚Äôs foundational work .

We believe that separating the semantic representation from the syntactic not only manages the two complex layers rather than creating a single layer, but also provides a more amenable representation for logic deduction tasks.

Our goal is to develop a single integrated system that uses a first-order logic solver using logical programming. With this goal in mind and the purpose of attaining high accuracy, we develop a formal semantic parsing algorithm that is driven by computational logic to transform the premises and candidate statements into first-order logic constructs. Another benefit of our methodology is the ability to answer queries related to the premises without access to the answer choices.

We build a computational linguistic system ‚Äì Formal Semantic Logic Inferer (FSLI), which embodies a semantic parsing algorithm that tightly integrates with a logic inference engine. FSLI is illustrated in Figure 1.

We model the semantics of a sentence using compositional formal semantic rules as introduced by Heim and Kratzer (1998) built upon Partee (1975)‚Äôs contributions to Montague (1970) Grammar. The result of applying formal semantic rules using word denotations is a logical formula for the sentence. In compositional formal semantics, a context ùíû\mathcal{C} is employed to interpret context-dependent expressions such as who the speaker is ‚ÄòI‚Äô, the place ‚Äòhere‚Äô, and the time ‚Äònow‚Äô. In our system, the context ùíû\mathcal{C} is represented as mapping between the entity label and the entity description an is dynamically populated. Context is not to be confused with coreference that is used to handle pronouns ‚Äì which do not exist in the dataset Sukthanker et al. (2020).

We algorithmize the compositional formal semantic rules, producing a procedure that converts a sentence into its logical representation using lambda expressions. Furthermore, we develop an approach to semantic parsing that populates the context with the entities in a dynamic manner. This is needed for logic deduction tasks as the entities are at the core of the problems and need to be extracted as the parser parses each sentence.

Our formal semantic parsing algorithm operates at four levels: word, sentence, paragraph, and problem. The word denotation extraction algorithm uses templates based on part of speech and named entity extraction to deduce the word‚Äôs logical representation (denotation).

The sentence-level semantic parser applies the formal semantic rules, uses the sentence‚Äôs syntax tree representation to combine the word denotations to the sentence-level denotation, and dynamically extracts the entities in the process.

The paragraph-level parser keeps track of the entities introduced in each sentence and passes them along to the following sentence in the paragraph. The problem-level semantic parser passes the entities introduced in the premises to the statements when arriving at their semantic denotation.

Srivastava et al. (2023)‚Äôs BIG-bench is a comprehensive benchmark comprised of various tasks designed to assess the performance and limitations of large language models. This includes a logical-deduction task555https://github.com/google/BIG-bench which employs linear arrangement as the relationship among entities.

The logical_deduction benchmark is comprised of three parts: sequences with three entities, five entities, and seven entities. The gradual increase in the number of entities introduces three difficulty levels to test since more entities introduce more relationships to keep track of.

The premises introduce the rules of entity ordering, while the candidate statements contain assertions that could be either true or false, along with their corresponding truth values (true = 1, false = 0).

Based on the programmatic construction of the dataset, the rules constrain the ordering entirely, so there is only one statement that is correct. These benchmark properties help test the semantic parsing algorithm, assuming that the logic model is sound. If the parsing is correct, we assume that the system should give the correct answer.

subsections.title: 4.1 Text Preprocessing
paragraphs: We use the Stanford Stanza pipeline Qi et al. (2020) to conduct text preprocessing and constituency syntactic parsing. Text preprocessing consists of part of speech (PoS) tagging, lemmatization, morphosyntactic feature tagging, and named entity recognition.

Lemmatization Jurafsky and Martin (2023) is the process of extracting the base form of a word (lemma). Dealing with base forms of words is sufficient for our purposes as the tense and other morphosyntactic attributes are of no consequence.

We center parsing on constituency parse for use in compositional formal semantics and syntax. The PoS tagging informs the semantic denotations of the leaves, and the daughters of a node combine to give the denotation of the node. In compositional formal semantics Heim and Kratzer (1998), the syntax is represented by a phrase structure grammar parse (constituency parse). We use the NLTK python library Bird et al. (2009) to binarize the constituency parse to Chomsky normal form (CNF) Chomsky (1959), which requires each constituent to be comprised of one or two parts.

Coreference, which is generally used for pronouns, is not applicable to our case. We handle contextual information pertaining to entities by dynamically populating a context that contains the entity description and its label as described in Section 5.2.

In order to map linear ordering-related phrasing in natural language text to the defined logic predicates, we create a translation table of the most common phrasing for linear ordering to the predicates. The keyword translations are related to direction, speed, expense, time, popularity, quantity, and performance. This listing is extrapolated from 10% of the data and extended using world knowledge to encompass as many phrases related to linear ordering as possible. Table 3 in Appendix A enumerates some of the phrases.

Compositional formal semantics Heim and Kratzer (1998) is a logical approach to semantics that labels words according to their first-order lambda logic notation. The logic representation of a sentence is determined by combining the logic representations of its constituents using the structure of the syntactic parse.

Logic representation of a word, phrase, or sentence is referred to as the denotation in compositional formal semantics. A denotation consists of two parts: first-order lambda logic notation and the semantic type. ‚ü¶Y‚üß\llbracket\textrm{Y}\rrbracket represents a denotation of Y where Y could be a word, phrase, or sentence.

Semantic types indicate whether the sentence, phrase, or word represents an entity, a truth value, or a functional type. An entity is represented with ee, and a truth value is represented with tt. ee and tt are the basic semantic types that can be combined to create functional types. A functional type ‚ü®œÉ,œÑ‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\sigma$,$\tau$}}\rangle where œÉ\sigma and œÑ\tau can be a basic type or a functional type. An entity is an object such as a noun. Examples of entities include a green apple, a blue banana, and Harry.
We generate denotations for a problem at three levels: word level, sentence level, and paragraph level. Lower-level denotations inform the denotations at the higher level.

subsections.title: 5.1 Word Level Semantic Denotation
paragraphs: In order to reduce the ambiguity of word denotation, we have one denotation per word/word category based on the most probable representation given the domain. For example, in our case, verbs are likely to be transitive verbs. We use predicates in our representations to allow semantic parsing output to be inputted to the logic inference system with little to no modifications. A predicate is a function that can take in one or more inputs. For example, b‚Äãe‚Äãf‚Äão‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äã(a,b)before(a,b) means a is before b.

We extend the denotation types to include two cross-categorial types: ‚ü®Œº,Œº‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\mu$,$\mu$}}\rangle and ‚ü®f,Œº‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$f$,$\mu$}}\rangle. Œº\mu is a placeholder type that could be of any semantic type, and ff is a placeholder type of a functional semantic type. They are used to allow a variety of input types rather than restricting it to a single type for word denotation or introducing ambiguity by having more than one possible denotation for a word.

Focusing on the part of speech categories: noun, adjective, verb, and propernoun; we use templates for the denotations. We use the lemma of a word as part of the logic representation. The templates are:

1. noun / adjective : (Œª‚Äãx\lambda x: l‚Äãe‚Äãm‚Äãm‚Äãa‚Äã(x)lemma(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) which means that a noun is a function of type ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle which takes as input an entity and returns a truth value.

2. verb : (Œª‚Äãx\lambda x: Œª‚Äãy\lambda y: l‚Äãe‚Äãm‚Äãm‚Äãa‚Äã(y,x)lemma(y,x), ‚ü®e,‚ü®e,t‚ü©‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$}}\rangle) which means that a verb is a function of type ‚ü®e,‚ü®e,t‚ü©‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$}}\rangle which takes as input an entity and returns a function which takes as input an entity and returns a truth value.

3. propernoun : (Œª‚Äãx\lambda x: l‚Äãe‚Äãm‚Äãm‚Äãa‚Äã(x)lemma(x), ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©, ec‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$, ec}}\rangle) which means that a propernoun is a function of type ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©, ec‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$, ec}}\rangle, which takes as input a function of type ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle and creates an entity using the input function.

We have introduced a special denotation type for propernouns ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©,ec‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$,ec}}\rangle. This follows the syntactic representation that a propernoun could be part of a determiner phrase with a hidden determiner Abney (1987). Keywords (kw) which represent sequential rules such as ‚Äòbefore‚Äô, ‚Äòafter‚Äô and ‚Äònext‚Äô are represented as : (‚ÄúŒª‚Äãx\lambda x: Œª‚Äãy\lambda y: kw(y,x)", ‚ü®e,‚ü®e,t‚ü©‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$}}\rangle). Where nn is the number which the numeric word represents such as ‚Äòone‚Äô = 1, ‚Äòthird‚Äô = 3.

p‚Äão‚Äãs‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äão‚Äãnposition is a predicate that starts counting from 1 with special keywords first and last. The position predicate assumes that the entities are in a unidirectional line, which is represented as a list that starts indexing at 1, and index(x) represents the index of entity x in the list. p‚Äão‚Äãs‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äão‚Äãn‚Äã(x,first,n)position(x,\textbf{first},n) means i‚Äãn‚Äãd‚Äãe‚Äãx‚Äã(x)index(x) = nn with first referring to the start of the entity list. p‚Äão‚Äãs‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äão‚Äãn‚Äã(x,last,n)position(x,\textbf{last},n) means i‚Äãn‚Äãd‚Äãe‚Äãx‚Äã(x)index(x) = NN - nn where NN is the number of entities introduced in the text; with last referring to the end of the entity list.

subsections.title: 5.2 Sentence Level Semantic Parser
paragraphs: We address semantic parsing in a rule-based manner, which models the rules introduced in Compositional Formal Semantics. The denotations of the words are derived using the PoS and NER tags informed using the combination rules.

A context ùíû\mathcal{C} maps between the entity description and its label. The context is updated when the semantic parser encounters a new entity. In the context ùíû\mathcal{C} = {hh : ‚Äòharry(h)‚Äô, rr: ‚Äòron(r)‚Äô}, ‚ÄòHarry‚Äô has the label hh and ‚ÄòRon‚Äô has label rr.

In order to identify the entities and use them dynamically across the text, we have a special denotation for the definite article (the) and the indefinite articles (a, an) ‚Äì described in Section 5.2.1. This implementation allows us to populate the context (ùíû\mathcal{C}) as we come across a new entity in each sentence in the text. In addition, by populating the context dynamically with the entities, we are introducing a novel approach to extracting entities that might not be captured using NER. We are also operating under the assumption that no two distinct entities have the same description.

Section 5.2.2 provides an illustrative example of how to populate the context and extract the denotation of each sentence in a paragraph using the formal semantic rules. Let ùíü\mathcal{D} be the denotation dictionary, which contains the denotation of the words in the sentence:

Function Application (FA): If X is a branching node that has two daughters - Y and Z - and if ‚ü¶Y‚üß\llbracket\textrm{Y}\rrbracket is a function whose domain contains ‚ü¶Z‚üß\llbracket\textrm{Z}\rrbracket, then ‚ü¶X‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$X$}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶Y‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$Y$}\rrbracket(‚ü¶Z‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$Z$}\rrbracket)

‚ü¶the‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the}\rrbracket = (‚ÄòŒª‚Äãf:g‚Äãe‚Äãt‚ÄãE‚Äãn‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(f,ùíû)\lambda f:getEntity(f,\mathcal{C})‚Äô, ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©, e‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$, e}}\rangle)

c‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äãe‚ÄãE‚Äãn‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äãt‚ÄãycreateEntity is a predicate that takes in a description, creates a label for the new entity, places it in the context ùíû\mathcal{C}, and returns the label as the denotation of the entity.
g‚Äãe‚Äãt‚ÄãE‚Äãn‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äãt‚ÄãygetEntity is a predicate that takes in a description and retrieves the label of the entity from the context ùíû\mathcal{C}.

Using the sentences in the following paragraph, we illustrate how to populate the context and extract the denotation of each sentence during tree traversal bottom-up right to left. The paragraph is: ‚ÄòA pink monkey is eating an apple. The apple is tasty.‚Äô.

The syntax tree of each sentence is traversed from right to left to derive the denotation of the sentence and populate the context. This direction is advantageous because nouns are encountered before their adjectives and related verbs. The syntax trees are in Figure 2.
Let ùíû\mathcal{C} be the context which starts empty. The denotation of the first sentence ‚ÄòA pink monkey is eating an apple‚Äô is derived as follows:

1. ùíû\mathcal{C} = {}, ‚ü¶apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{apple}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:a‚Äãp‚Äãp‚Äãl‚Äãe‚Äã(x)\lambda x:apple(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶an‚üß\llbracket\textrm{an}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãf:c‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äãe‚ÄãE‚Äãn‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(f,ùíû)\lambda f:createEntity(f,\mathcal{C}), ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©, e‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$, e}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{an apple}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶an‚üß\llbracket\textrm{an}\rrbracket(‚ü¶apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{apple}\rrbracket) = a , ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a)} (by FA)

2. ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a)}, ‚ü¶is‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãf:f\lambda f:f, ‚ü®Œº,Œº‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\mu$,$\mu$}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶eating‚üß\llbracket\textrm{eating}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,x)\lambda x:\lambda y:eat(y,x), ‚ü®e,‚ü®e,t‚ü©‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶is eating‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶is‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is}\rrbracket(‚ü¶eating‚üß\llbracket\textrm{eating}\rrbracket) = (Œª‚Äãx:Œª‚Äãy:s‚Äãi‚Äãn‚Äãg‚Äã(y,x)\lambda x:\lambda y:sing(y,x), ‚ü®e,‚ü®e,t‚ü©‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$}}\rangle) (by FA and Œ≤\beta-reduction)

3. ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a)}, ‚ü¶monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{monkey}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:m‚Äão‚Äãn‚Äãk‚Äãe‚Äãy‚Äã(x)\lambda x:monkey(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶pink‚üß\llbracket\textrm{pink}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:p‚Äãi‚Äãn‚Äãk‚Äã(x)\lambda x:pink(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{pink monkey}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx\lambda x: p‚Äãi‚Äãn‚Äãk‚Äã(x)pink(x) and m‚Äão‚Äãn‚Äãk‚Äãe‚Äãy‚Äã(x)monkey(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by PM)

4. ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a)}, ‚ü¶a‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãf:c‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äãe‚ÄãE‚Äãn‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(f,ùíû)\lambda f:createEntity(f,\mathcal{C}), ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©, e‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$, e}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶a pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a pink monkey}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶a‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a}\rrbracket(‚ü¶pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{pink monkey}\rrbracket) = p , ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)} (by FA, step (c))

5. ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}, ‚ü¶is eating‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,x)\lambda x:\lambda y:eat(y,x), ‚ü®e,‚ü®e,t‚ü©‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$}}\rangle) (by step (b)) ‚ü¶an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{an apple}\rrbracket = a (by step (a)) ‚ü¶is eating an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating an apple}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,a)\lambda y:eat(y,a), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by FA and Œ≤\beta-reduction)

6. ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}, ‚ü¶is eating an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating an apple}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,a)\lambda y:eat(y,a), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by step 5) ‚ü¶a pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a pink monkey}\rrbracket = p (by step (d)) ‚ü¶a pink monkey is eating an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a pink monkey is eating an apple}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶is eating an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating an apple}\rrbracket(‚ü¶a pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a pink monkey}\rrbracket) = ((Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,a)\lambda y:eat(y,a), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle)(p) = (e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(p,a)eat(p,a), t) (by FA and Œ≤\beta-reduction)

ùíû\mathcal{C} = {}, ‚ü¶apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{apple}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:a‚Äãp‚Äãp‚Äãl‚Äãe‚Äã(x)\lambda x:apple(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶an‚üß\llbracket\textrm{an}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãf:c‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äãe‚ÄãE‚Äãn‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(f,ùíû)\lambda f:createEntity(f,\mathcal{C}), ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©, e‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$, e}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{an apple}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶an‚üß\llbracket\textrm{an}\rrbracket(‚ü¶apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{apple}\rrbracket) = a , ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a)} (by FA)

ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a)}, ‚ü¶is‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãf:f\lambda f:f, ‚ü®Œº,Œº‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\mu$,$\mu$}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶eating‚üß\llbracket\textrm{eating}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,x)\lambda x:\lambda y:eat(y,x), ‚ü®e,‚ü®e,t‚ü©‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶is eating‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶is‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is}\rrbracket(‚ü¶eating‚üß\llbracket\textrm{eating}\rrbracket) = (Œª‚Äãx:Œª‚Äãy:s‚Äãi‚Äãn‚Äãg‚Äã(y,x)\lambda x:\lambda y:sing(y,x), ‚ü®e,‚ü®e,t‚ü©‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$}}\rangle) (by FA and Œ≤\beta-reduction)

ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a)}, ‚ü¶monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{monkey}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:m‚Äão‚Äãn‚Äãk‚Äãe‚Äãy‚Äã(x)\lambda x:monkey(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶pink‚üß\llbracket\textrm{pink}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:p‚Äãi‚Äãn‚Äãk‚Äã(x)\lambda x:pink(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{pink monkey}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx\lambda x: p‚Äãi‚Äãn‚Äãk‚Äã(x)pink(x) and m‚Äão‚Äãn‚Äãk‚Äãe‚Äãy‚Äã(x)monkey(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by PM)

ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a)}, ‚ü¶a‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãf:c‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äãe‚ÄãE‚Äãn‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(f,ùíû)\lambda f:createEntity(f,\mathcal{C}), ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©, e‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$, e}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶a pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a pink monkey}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶a‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a}\rrbracket(‚ü¶pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{pink monkey}\rrbracket) = p , ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)} (by FA, step (c))

ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}, ‚ü¶is eating‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,x)\lambda x:\lambda y:eat(y,x), ‚ü®e,‚ü®e,t‚ü©‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$}}\rangle) (by step (b)) ‚ü¶an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{an apple}\rrbracket = a (by step (a)) ‚ü¶is eating an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating an apple}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,a)\lambda y:eat(y,a), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by FA and Œ≤\beta-reduction)

ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}, ‚ü¶is eating an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating an apple}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,a)\lambda y:eat(y,a), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by step 5) ‚ü¶a pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a pink monkey}\rrbracket = p (by step (d)) ‚ü¶a pink monkey is eating an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a pink monkey is eating an apple}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶is eating an apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is eating an apple}\rrbracket(‚ü¶a pink monkey‚üß\llbracket\textrm{a pink monkey}\rrbracket) = ((Œª‚Äãy:e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(y,a)\lambda y:eat(y,a), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle)(p) = (e‚Äãa‚Äãt‚Äã(p,a)eat(p,a), t) (by FA and Œ≤\beta-reduction)

The denotation of the second sentence ‚ÄòThe apple is tasty‚Äô is derived as follows. The context ùíû\mathcal{C} will be the final context derived from the first sentence ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}:

1. ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}, ‚ü¶apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{apple}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:a‚Äãp‚Äãp‚Äãl‚Äãe‚Äã(x)\lambda x:apple(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶the‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãf\lambda f: g‚Äãe‚Äãt‚ÄãE‚Äãn‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(f,ùíû)getEntity(f,\mathcal{C}), ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©, e‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$, e}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶the apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the apple}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶the‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the}\rrbracket(‚ü¶apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{apple}\rrbracket) = a (by FA)

3. ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}, ‚ü¶the apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the apple}\rrbracket = a (by step (a)) ‚ü¶is tasty‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is tasty}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:t‚Äãa‚Äãs‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(x)\lambda x:tasty(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by step (b)) ‚ü¶the apple is tasty‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the apple is tasty}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶is tasty‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is tasty}\rrbracket(‚ü¶the apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the apple}\rrbracket) = (Œª‚Äãx:t‚Äãa‚Äãs‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(x)\lambda x:tasty(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle)(a) = (tasty(a), t) (by FA and Œ≤\beta-reduction)

ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}, ‚ü¶apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{apple}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:a‚Äãp‚Äãp‚Äãl‚Äãe‚Äã(x)\lambda x:apple(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶the‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãf\lambda f: g‚Äãe‚Äãt‚ÄãE‚Äãn‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(f,ùíû)getEntity(f,\mathcal{C}), ‚ü®‚ü®e,t‚ü©, e‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle$, e}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶the apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the apple}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶the‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the}\rrbracket(‚ü¶apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{apple}\rrbracket) = a (by FA)

ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}, ‚ü¶is‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãf:f\lambda f:f, ‚ü®Œº,Œº‚ü©\langle\textrm{{$\mu$,$\mu$}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶tasty‚üß\llbracket\textrm{tasty}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:t‚Äãa‚Äãs‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(x)\lambda x:tasty(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by TN) ‚ü¶is tasty‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is tasty}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶is‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is}\rrbracket(‚ü¶tasty‚üß\llbracket\textrm{tasty}\rrbracket) = (Œª‚Äãx:t‚Äãa‚Äãs‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(x)\lambda x:tasty(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by FA and Œ≤\beta-reduction)

ùíû\mathcal{C} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}, ‚ü¶the apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the apple}\rrbracket = a (by step (a)) ‚ü¶is tasty‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is tasty}\rrbracket = (Œª‚Äãx:t‚Äãa‚Äãs‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(x)\lambda x:tasty(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle) (by step (b)) ‚ü¶the apple is tasty‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the apple is tasty}\rrbracket = ‚ü¶is tasty‚üß\llbracket\textrm{is tasty}\rrbracket(‚ü¶the apple‚üß\llbracket\textrm{the apple}\rrbracket) = (Œª‚Äãx:t‚Äãa‚Äãs‚Äãt‚Äãy‚Äã(x)\lambda x:tasty(x), ‚ü®e,t‚ü©\langle\textrm{{e,t}}\rangle)(a) = (tasty(a), t) (by FA and Œ≤\beta-reduction)

subsections.title: 5.3 Paragraph Level Semantic Parser
paragraphs: A paragraph is the input to the semantic parser, and the output is the denotation of each of the sentences and the context. A context is a dictionary that contains the label and description of each entity.
Let ùíûi‚Äãn\mathcal{C}_{in} = {} be the input context. Let the paragraph PP = [X1X_{1}, X2X_{2} ‚Ä¶, XnX_{n}] where nn is the number of sentences in the paragraph.

The context outputted after getting the denotation of sentence ii is ùíûi\mathcal{C}_{i} and the denotation of the sentence ‚ü¶Xi‚üß\llbracket\textrm{X${}_{i}$}\rrbracket = RiR_{i} using the input context ùíûi‚àí1\mathcal{C}_{i-1} which is the output context of ‚ü¶Xi-1‚üß\llbracket\textrm{X${}_{i-1}$}\rrbracket.
‚ü¶P‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$P$}\rrbracket = [R1R_{1}, R2R_{2} ‚Ä¶, RnR_{n}] with output context ùíûo‚Äãu‚Äãt\mathcal{C}_{out} = ùíûn\mathcal{C}_{n}.

Using the example in Section 5.2.2, we illustrate the denotation of a paragraph. PP = [‚ÄòA pink monkey is eating an apple.‚Äô, ‚ÄòThe apple is tasty.‚Äô]. X1X_{1} = ‚ÄòA pink monkey is eating an apple.‚Äô X2X_{2} = ‚ÄòThe apple is tasty.‚Äô
‚ü¶P‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$P$}\rrbracket = [‚ü¶X1‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$X_{1}$}\rrbracket, ‚ü¶X2‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$X_{2}$}\rrbracket] = [R1R_{1}, R2R_{2}] = [(eat(p, a), t), (tasty(a), t)]
ùíûo‚Äãu‚Äãt\mathcal{C}_{out} = ùíû2\mathcal{C}_{2} = {a: apple(a), p: pink(p) and monkey(p)}

subsections.title: 5.4 Problem Level Semantic Parser
paragraphs: A BIG-bench logic deduction problem contains the premise and the candidate statements, with the underlying question being ‚Äòwhat is true?‚Äô.

Let ùíûi‚Äãn\mathcal{C}_{in} = {} be the input context. Let ‚Ñí\mathcal{L} = (PP, QQ, AA) be the problem where PP is the premise that introduces the set of linear ordering rules, QQ is the related question ‚Äòwhat is true?‚Äô, and AA is the candidate statements related to the premise. Let nn be the number of sentences in the premise, mm the number of sentences in the question, and ww the number of sentences of the candidate statements. The denotations of the premise, question, and candidate statements are computed using the paragraph-level semantic parser detailed in Section 5.3:
‚ü¶P‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$P$}\rrbracket = [Rp‚Äã1R_{p1}, ‚Ä¶, Rp‚ÄãnR_{pn}] with output context ùíûp‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äãm‚Äãi‚Äãs‚Äãe\mathcal{C}_{premise} and input context ùíûi‚Äãn\mathcal{C}_{in}.
‚ü¶Q‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$Q$}\rrbracket = RqR_{q} with output context ùíûq‚Äãu‚Äãe‚Äãs‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äão‚Äãn\mathcal{C}_{question} and input context ùíûp‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äãm‚Äãi‚Äãs‚Äãe\mathcal{C}_{premise}.

‚ü¶A‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$A$}\rrbracket = [Ra‚Äã1R_{a1}, ‚Ä¶, Ra‚ÄãwR_{aw}] with output context ùíûc‚Äãh‚Äão‚Äãi‚Äãc‚Äãe‚Äãs\mathcal{C}_{choices} and input context ùíûq‚Äãu‚Äãe‚Äãs‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äão‚Äãn\mathcal{C}_{question}.

‚ü¶‚Ñí‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$\mathcal{L}$}\rrbracket = (‚ü¶P‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$P$}\rrbracket, ‚ü¶Q‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$Q$}\rrbracket, ‚ü¶A‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$A$}\rrbracket), Entities = labels of the entities in ùíûc‚Äãh‚Äão‚Äãi‚Äãc‚Äãe‚Äãs\mathcal{C}_{choices}.
‚ü¶P‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$P$}\rrbracket = [Rp‚Äã1R_{p1}, ‚Ä¶, Rp‚ÄãnR_{pn}] with output context ùíûp‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äãm‚Äãi‚Äãs‚Äãe\mathcal{C}_{premise} and input context ùíûi‚Äãn\mathcal{C}_{in}.
‚ü¶Q‚üß\llbracket\textrm{$Q$}\rrbracket = RqR_{q} with output context ùíûq‚Äãu‚Äãe‚Äãs‚Äãt‚Äãi‚Äão‚Äãn\mathcal{C}_{question} and input context ùíûp‚Äãr‚Äãe‚Äãm‚Äãi‚Äãs‚Äãe\mathcal{C}_{premise}.

We utilize SWI-Prolog Wielemaker et al. (2012), a declarative programming language, in order to specify the conditions to infer the solution. We define the main SWI-Prolog predicate (l‚Äãi‚Äãn‚Äãe‚Äãa‚Äãr‚ÄãO‚Äãr‚Äãd‚Äãe‚Äãr‚ÄãI‚Äãn‚ÄãflinearOrderInf), which takes in the constraints and the entities of a problem and returns a model ‚Ñ≥\mathcal{M} and can take in a query to infer the correct answer. A model ‚Ñ≥\mathcal{M} is :
The main predicate constrains the entities to positions or relative positions in the list based on the constraints, allowing the resulting model to query based on these constraints.

The semantic denotation of each candidate statement is passed into the model ‚Ñ≥\mathcal{M} as a query to infer whether the statement is true or false. The set of rules is extracted based on the semantic parse of the premises. The listed entities are the labels of entities in the output context of a problem ‚Ñí\mathcal{L}: ùíûùêã\mathcal{C}_{\mathbf{L}}.

We model a linear ordering problem by defining predicates for the Constraint Logic Programming over Finite Domains (CLP(FD)) SWI-Prolog library Triska (2012). The representation utilizes integer indexing as well as transitivity and other typical axioms for an ordering domain. For entities A and B, and number n, the ordering predicates are: before(A, B), succeed(A, B), not_(constraint), first(A), last(A), position(A, first, n), position(A, last, n).

To solve the problem ‚ÑíPB‚ÄãB\mathcal{L}_{P_{BB}} introduced in Section 5.4, the system creates the logic inference model ‚Ñ≥B‚ÄãB\mathcal{M}_{BB} based on Equation (1) and semantic parsing algorithm output on ‚ÑíPB‚ÄãB\mathcal{L}_{P_{BB}} shown in Section 5.4. The output of FSLI using the model ‚Ñ≥B‚ÄãB\mathcal{M}_{BB} on ‚ÑíPB‚ÄãB\mathcal{L}_{P_{BB}} is [0, 1, 0]; which means the second choice is true and the others are false.

We use the BIG-bench benchmark Srivastava et al. (2023) to asses FSLI‚Äôs semantic parsing proficiency and ensure the soundness of its logical inferences since is only one valid sequence for the arrangement of entities.

We tested the following LLMs: GPT-4, PALM-2 and LLAMA-2-13b-chat OpenAI et al. (2023); Anil et al. (2023); Touvron et al. (2023). Appendix B presents examples of LLM responses to BBLD problems and illustrates the prompt used.

GPT-4 gets the highest score among LLMs, scoring an accuracy of 67.06% on the BIG-bench logical_deduction (BBLD) 666https://github.com/google/BIG-bench/tree/main/BIG-bench/, but fails to scale nicely with large decreases in accuracy across the subtasks of BBLD on three entities (93%), five entities (65.6%), and seven entities (57%). The large difference in accuracy (93% vs 65.6%) indicates the challenge GPT-4 faces in logical deduction.

The accuracy scores reported in Table 2 indicate that our model (FSLI) ‚Äì with a score of 100% ‚Äì outperforms LLMs and Logic-LM Pan et al. (2023) on BBLD. Table 2 reports the score of Logic-LM of 87.63% on a subset of 300 examples in BIG-bench logical_deduction as presented in their paper Pan et al. (2023). The reported score of GPT-4 using chain of thought prompting on the 300 examples is 75.25% Pan et al. (2023).
The accuracy, interpretability, explainability and reproducibility of FSLI are all evidences in favour of adopting our methodology for natural language logical deduction tasks.
We have developed a Formal Semantic Logic Inferer (FSLI) system that consists of a semantic parsing algorithm tightly integrated with the logic inference engine.

We use the BIG-bench logical_deduction benchmark to assess FSLI‚Äôs semantic parsing proficiency and ensure the soundness of its logical inferences since there is only one valid sequence for the arrangement of entities.
FSLI attains 100% accuracy in solving BIG-bench logical_deduction problems compared to performance of systems based on general-purpose large language models that rely on patterns.
This suggests the feasibility of linguistic-based algorithms for deterministic problems that require logic deduction to build a systematic model of relationships that are consistent among entities.
FSLI can be expanded to solve more complex logical deduction problems related to linear ordering that require the determination of multiple valid orderings and those that do not provide choices.

The logic-driven semantic layer developed offers the future possibility of utilizing such a lambda calculus-based layer to handle problems that may be framed as constraint satisfaction problems, such as the N-Queens problem posed in natural language.
This research illustrates the promise and important future steps toward more rich and powerful logical-based reasoning for natural language problems.
